questions.q: What did the Maltese cross experiment with cathode rays demonstrate?
options: That cathode rays carry a positive charge
That cathode rays travel in straight lines, since a sharp shadow of the cross appeared on the glass
That cathode rays are slowed by metal
That cathode rays are a form of sound
questions.explanation: A sharp shadow of the metal cross appeared on the glowing far wall, showing the rays were blocked by the metal and travelled in straight lines, just as light would cast a shadow.

questions.q: Why did Hertz initially conclude that cathode rays were uncharged waves when he passed them between charged plates?
options: The rays sped up between the plates
The rays were not deflected by the electric field, because leftover gas in his tube was ionised and short-circuited the field
The rays glowed brighter near the positive plate
The rays were absorbed by the plates
questions.explanation: Hertz saw no deflection, but the cause was poor vacuum: cathode rays ionised the leftover gas, and those ions piled onto the plates and cancelled the electric field. With nothing pushing them, the rays went straight — hiding their charge.

questions.q: How did Thomson resolve the contradiction between Perrin's result (rays carry negative charge) and Hertz's (no electric deflection)?
options: He concluded Perrin was wrong
He produced a much better vacuum, removing the gas so the field was no longer cancelled; the rays then deflected toward the positive plate, proving they are negatively charged
He used a stronger magnet instead
He decided the rays were both waves and particles
questions.explanation: Thomson suspected leftover gas was the problem, so he pumped the tube to a far better vacuum. With little gas to ionise, the electric field survived and the beam bent toward the positive plate — confirming the rays are negatively charged.

questions.q: In Thomson's crossed-field (velocity selector) arrangement, when the electric and magnetic forces balance so the beam is undeflected, the speed of the particles is:
options: \(v = \dfrac{E}{B}\)
\(v = \dfrac{B}{E}\)
\(v = \dfrac{1}{EB}\)
questions.explanation: Balancing \(qE = qvB\), the charge cancels, giving \(v = E/B\). The speed depends only on the two field strengths the experimenter sets — independent of charge and mass.

questions.q: When the magnetic field acts alone and curves the beam into a circle, which quantity is providing the centripetal force?
options: Gravity
The electric force
The magnetic force on the moving charge
Air resistance
questions.explanation: A magnetic force acts at right angles to the motion, so it cannot change the speed — only bend the path into a circle. Setting the magnetic force equal to the centripetal force (\(qvB = mv^2/r\)) and using the known speed yields the charge-to-mass ratio.

questions.q: Why could Thomson's experiment only give the charge-to-mass ratio, not the charge or the mass separately?
options: His instruments were too crude
The charge and mass always appear welded together in the equations — a stronger force bends the beam more, but more mass resists it — so only the ratio q/m drops out
He never measured the magnetic field
The particles had no mass
questions.explanation: Charge sets how strongly the field grips the particle, while mass (inertia) resists being pushed. In the algebra they never separate, so only the ratio \(q/m\) emerges. A separate measurement of the charge (later Millikan) is needed to prise them apart.

questions.q: Thomson found the charge-to-mass ratio of cathode-ray particles was about 1800 times that of the hydrogen ion. He argued this meant the particles have a tiny mass (rather than a huge charge) because:
options: They glowed brighter than hydrogen ions
They travelled extremely far through gas before being stopped — a hugely charged particle would interact strongly and be stopped almost at once
They were attracted to the cathode
They had the same colour as hydrogen
questions.explanation: Lenard's results showed the particles slipped through matter for enormous distances. A particle with gigantic charge would grab every atom it passed and stop quickly; gliding through means it interacts weakly — so the explanation is a tiny mass, not a huge charge.

questions.q: What was the crucial evidence that the cathode-ray particle is a universal constituent of all matter?
options: The rays always glowed green
The charge-to-mass ratio came out the same regardless of which gas filled the tube or which metal formed the cathode
The rays only appeared with platinum electrodes
The particles changed mass with each different gas
questions.explanation: Thomson varied the gas (air, hydrogen, carbon dioxide) and the cathode metal (aluminium, platinum), yet the same charge-to-mass ratio appeared every time. The particle could not be a fragment of any particular substance — it is a building block present in all matter.

questions.q: How does Millikan's oil-drop experiment complete the picture begun by Thomson?
options: It measured the speed of the electron
It measured the charge on a single electron directly, which combined with Thomson's q/m ratio gives the electron's mass
It proved cathode rays are waves
It measured the size of the atom
questions.explanation: Millikan suspended charged oil drops by balancing the electric force against gravity (\(qE = mg\)) and found charge comes in whole-number multiples of one elementary charge. Dividing that charge by Thomson's q/m ratio gives the electron mass (~\(9\times10^{-31}\) kg).

questions.q: What model of the atom did Thomson propose after discovering the electron, and how was it overturned?
options: The nuclear model, overturned by the Bohr model
A model of negative electrons embedded in a sphere of positive charge ('plum pudding'), overturned by the Geiger-Marsden gold-foil experiment showing a dense nucleus
The quark model, overturned by Chadwick
A solar-system model, confirmed by Rutherford
questions.explanation: Thomson pictured electrons embedded in a smeared sphere of positive charge. The Geiger-Marsden experiment found alpha particles bouncing back, impossible if charge were spread softly — so the positive charge must be concentrated in a tiny dense nucleus, overturning the model.
